speaker: interviewee
text: [TEST TONE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Good morning. It's March 24th, 1996. The interviewee is-- is Professor Alfred Steer and the interviewer is myself, Bruce Einhorn. The city is Washington, District of Columbia, and the language of the day is English. [GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: One more time. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The date is March 24th, 1996. The subject of the interview is Professor Alfred Steer. The interviewer is myself, Bruce Einhorn. The place of the interview is Washington, District of Columbia. And the language of the interview will be English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, good morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sir. How are you?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm well. Could you just, for the record, please state your full name and spell it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alfred Gilbert Steer, Jr. And I always have to spell the Steer. Everybody tries to make me a Stewart or a Steiner or something. It's Steer like beef, or steering a car.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Where are you living now, Professor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Athens, Georgia. I'm a retired professor from the University of Georgia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And professor of what?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was head of the Department of Germanic and Slavic Languages.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was down at University of Georgia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, sir.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And where were you born, sir?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lansdowne, Pennsylvania, which is a suburb of Philadelphia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] 1913. Which makes me ancient as hell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm a junior. It was exactly the same as mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember when and where he was born, sir?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was born in Colerain-- I spell C-O-L-E-R-A-I-N-- Ohio, in 1877.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did he do for a living in the States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm retired. I live off of my, my, my pensions.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your dad, what did he do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had two-- he was a-- he owned a business in Philadelphia for dairyman's supply. You know, milking machines and cream separators and all that sort of stuff. And he sold that through the Philadelphia area. Then a big conglomerate bought him out and he turned to, to selling life insurance. And he spent about the last 20 years of his life selling life insurance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when your father passed away?

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was living where at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was living in, in, in Lansdowne. Mhm. Still.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother, what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Selma Elizabeth Taber, T-A-B-E-R, was her maiden name. And she was eight years younger than Dad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in Barnesville, Ohio.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how they met?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I wasn't around, obviously. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I don't. I can't tell you that. OK. Did she work outside the home during the marriage?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- no. She worked before. She was a teacher, and she taught in a number of private Quaker schools-- they were both very strong Quakers-- before she was married. But then she stopped that when she-- when she married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is she alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. She's dead. She, she died-- --in '77 at age 94.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I had-- I was the oldest. My next younger sister was year and a half younger. She's still alive in Boston, and she has seven-- count 'em, seven-- kids. Then came a brother four years younger than I, named John. He lives in Connecticut, had three kids. And then I had a baby sister 12 years younger than I, Mary Ellen, who lives in Bryn Mawr, PA, and is a widow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of your siblings participate in the Nuremberg Trials?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how long did you live in the Lansdowne area before you moved on in life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was born and brought up there. And then I went to college also in the Philadelphia area. Haverford, which is really a-- just a long commute, on the other side of the city. And I lived there-- it was essentially like I graduated from college. Then I moved away. I had an exchange-- --scholarship to Germany in '35 and '6. And after that, I never returned home again. I, I took a-- my master's degree at Duke University. I worked toward my PhD at Wisconsin.

speaker: interviewer
text: I taught at Washington and Lee for four years, and so forth. I was round and around. A what will I say? A random-- randomly-employed junior college academic teacher. What was it like growing up in Lansdowne? What kind of place was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was a-- I'd say middle class. Very definitely middle-class. We didn't have any really wealthy people. Now, the stylish side of Philadelphia is the Main Line. We were not Main Line. But-- And one of the low-- low-- low points of the Philadelphia suburbs was Darby, PA. Well, we were about 12 miles from Darby. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So middle class. Now, if you don't mind my asking, what was your family's religious background? I know you mentioned your mom was a Quaker.

speaker: interviewee
text: Quaker. Both, very strongly. Very strongly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you brought up in that atmosphere?

speaker: interviewee
text: I certainly was. I went to Quaker schools and we went to Quaker Meeting every Sunday and all that sort of thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went-- that was a private school also, Quaker school. Westtown School.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any particular career goals when you entered Haverford College?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] You're taking me way back, friend. I, I-- let me put it this way. While at Haverford I studied German and I was-- majored in German, and so on. And I was very much impressed by the literary criticism of some of the famous German scholars.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I thought, boy, I would like to be able to write a book like that. Or on that level, of that caliber. And I think that's when my-- what will I say? Ambition to become a literary scholar really began, while I was a student at Haverford. Now, whether-- what year it was, or when, I, I can't tell you. And what was your major, or concentration in college? Was it German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, German, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a minor in French. I also knew French quite well. That was-- I had a linguistic aptitude. I knew that, and I was simply capitalizing on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you receive your bachelor's degree at Haverford?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any particular politics in the '30s?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. [LAUGHS] I was very much impressed with Franklin Delano Roosevelt. And I had lived through this whole Depression business. And I remember, for instance, the Dust Bowl. I don't know if you recall, but great clouds of dust came over Philadelphia, PA from the Dust Bowl. I mean, we were very much caught up in this. And I-- I thought Roosevelt had a-- was a far better-- what will I say-- prospective leader than Hoover. Now, Hoover was a Quaker. And therefore, my parents adored him, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I announced that I was going to vote for Roosevelt, oh, I'm telling you. It was a family explosion. How could I do such a thing? Well I did it.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you mentioned you matriculated through Yale for a master's degree. When did you receive that master's?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not Yale.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry, Duke.

speaker: interviewee
text: Duke, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Duke, and when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was '37. Mhm. Two years later.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Why, why choose Duke? Why North Carolina?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --they had-- they had the money. I was in Germany and I was trying to figure, now I want to go and I want to get a PhD. This is my-- this a requirement. The sine qua non, you know? If you want to become anything in the academic world, you have to have your trade union card, namely a PhD.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. I had done some work for and summers at Wisconsin, but I sent a group of letters to various different places I knew which gave out graduate fellowships. And one of them was to Duke. And-- because they had a scholarship-- oh, friend, I think it was $600. That was a lot of money in those days. And so I got a reply. And they said, yes, they could-- they-- they would give it to me. And-- and I would indicate my reply, my acceptance, by simply cabling one word, 'yes.' [LAUGHS] That tickled the hell out of me. Saved me a lot of money. So I cabled 'yes,' and went down to Duke.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I believe you said a moment ago that between Haverford and Duke, you were in Germany. When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was '35 and '36.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was--

speaker: interviewee
text: And a little bit more.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the purpose of that trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was-- I was-- I had a fellowship. I had a post-graduate fellowship to study abroad anywhere-- --I wanted. And I chose, of course, Germany. [INAUDIBLE] And I spent 12 months in Germany and then another additional three months in, in Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Germany did you study?

speaker: interviewee
text: All-- well, primarily-- to begin with, Berlin. And then the-- that was for the summer of 1935. Then with the fall semester, I went to Jena, which is in middle-- middle Germany, as you probably are aware, and spent the two winters, winter and spring semesters there. Then at the end of that I toured Germany and went to-- went to France.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your impressions of Germany in the mid-'30s?

speaker: interviewee
text: I liked Germany. I don't know if you know the country. It's a beautiful country. Even today, even with this incredible overcrowding that they have there. I liked the basic Germans. But I was horrified at Hitler-ism. As a matter of fact, I was more than horrified, I, I was scared. I was afraid. I had read Toynbee's study of civilization. You're probably familiar with it. And I was convinced that he had it right. But that Nazism, if it were successful, would undermine this and destroy it. And that this must not be allowed to happen. And I was very scornful, of course, of their anti-Semitism, which I took at that point as simply a ploy to gain, how will I say, a majority vote from the German people and get into power. I didn't really see it in the terrible crime aspect, which we later came to recognize was behind it. I thought, simply-- there are plenty of anti-Semites in the US, always were, had been when I grew up. I was familiar with this, this was nothing new. But then I, I-- but what I saw was, this movement, if it was successful, could spell the end of Western civilization as we knew it. And this scared the hell out of me. And I came back from Germany-- I don't know if you remember. I was a great student Lincoln one point. When he first went to New Orleans, when he went down the river on that flatboat with his family's farm produce to sell them for abroad. Well, he came in close contact with slavery for the first time in his life. And he lived there for apparently two or three weeks before he got everything sold and went back home. And he is supposed to have said to his-- one of his neighbors went along with him. If I ever get a chance to hit that thing, slavery, I'm going to hit it hard. Well, we know what he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I felt exactly the same way about Nazism. When I came home from Europe in, in '36, in the fall of '36, I was on the old German Remember that famous luxury liner? Wow. I was, of course, down in the steerage, [LAUGHS] all I could afford.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I remember thinking about my year behind me, and the years ahead. And I remember saying to myself, well-- I-- I'm going to quote Lincoln here. If I ever get a chance to hit that thing, I'm going to hit it hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you came back and, and went to Duke, you, you studied there what, specifically?

speaker: interviewee
text: I studied well, German literature. And I wrote a doctor's-- excuse me, a master's thesis on-- on Gottfried Keller. You probably never heard of him. He was a Swiss short story writer, quite a decent one. Not really well-recognized in the English-speaking world at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I believe you also said you spent some time in Wisconsin. Was that as a student?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I originally planned to, to do my PhD work at Wisconsin, which had the best-- or was reputed to be the best-- German department of any-- any-- US-- US university at that point. And I went out there for two summers, I think, preparing to, to go for my PhD.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that before Duke or after Duke?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was after Duke.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: After Duke, I got a job teaching at Washington and Lee.

speaker: interviewer
text: For those, for those who might not know, where is Washington and Lee?

speaker: interviewee
text: Washington and Lee is in Lexington, Virginia. Named Washington and Lee because when George Washington left the army of the United States and became president, the state of Virginia gave him a monetary reward. It was something like $15,000, I've forgotten now what it was. It was a number of shares in a certain canal company. Anyway. And he refused to accept them. And instead he knew of this little struggling school up in the Shenandoah Valley called Liberty Academy or something. And he gave it to them. And they renamed themselves Washington College in honor of this gift. By the way, it's still there, and I have profited by income from it. But anyway. And then when-- when the Civil War was over, the question of what we're going to do with Robert E Lee. And the same little college offered him a position as president. And he accepted. And then after his death, they changed it to Washington and Lee University. It's-- it's a fine private university.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during what years did you teach there?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was '37 to '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you teach?

speaker: interviewee
text: I taught German, well-- and I also taught French and English. Will you believe it? Triple threat man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak French then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And Then when did you go off for your doctorate?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I, I put in, in the spring of 1941, for a fellowship to go to Wisconsin, study at Wisconsin. All right. There were two types of fellowship at Wisconsin at that point. There was the $400 number, and a $600 number. [LAUGHS] That may not sound like much today. But for, for you-- a single man, as a student, if you wanted to do a couple of jobs like waiting on tables, could get by, you see, for an academic year on $600.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, I was real tickled. I got a 600-bucker. You see? For me, all right. So this was for the fall of-- of '41. So I had also-- this is another angle-- decided, as I told you, when I came back from Europe, that I was going to do something about this Hitler business.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had read US history, and I became painfully aware that the US has a very bad history, or habit, of not preparing in advance for a war. And then when the war comes, boom. Everybody gets drafted and cannon fodder gets thrown onto the battlefield with minimum or no training, and they get slaughtered. And that the best way to avoid that-- as a, as a possible slaughteree, you see-- was to get some advanced training. Not only would I be more effective on the battlefield, but I would have a better chance of survival. And so I determined to get into the service of my choice before the war began. And I applied for a commission in the United States Navy. And [LAUGHS] a week after I got the acceptance from Wisconsin, the Navy said-- sent me that they accepted me. And they said, we are ordering you to active duty on the 20th of June, 1941. Well. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I wrote Wisconsin. I said, can I postpone my fellowship? And they said, no. But you can reapply. So I wrote the Navy and I called-- I wrote them I guess-- and said, can I-- can I postpone my entrance into the Navy? They said, no. You can resign your commission, in which case, we can assure you, you will be drafted at once. [LAUGHS] And your decision was?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] My decision was obvious. Now, that was the end of my academic studies for eight years-- seven years. There was a big hiatus there, the Navy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, why the Navy, Professor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why the Navy? I have to go into a little family history. The name Steer-- There's, there's a family tradition. The name Steer is supposedly due to the fact that the man who steered or piloted William the Conqueror across the Channel before he beat the British in the Battle of Hastings in 1066 AD was the original Steer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I don't know-- know anything about Viking ships, but the-- the Viking steering oar-- they didn't have a rudder-- steering oar was over the starboard side. By the way, I called starboard-- it's steerboard, you see? Starboard. And he would steer the ship from the steering oar. And at the same time, with his left-- that was his right hand-- with his left hand. He would handle a sheet. Now, the sheet, to you landlubbers, is the rope that controls the sails, you see? All right. So he was the, the pilot and the steersman and everything else. And if you look at the tapestry, what's that tapestry? The-- the famous tapestry that describes the invasion of England by-- anyway. Named for a French town. I can't think of it, that's terrible. You can see it. There's a picture of him, sitting there in the stern [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father told me this when-- when I was a boy. He said-- on my 12th birthday, he said, now, this is a family tradition. I was told this by my father on my 12th birthday, I'm telling you, I want you to tell your son on your 12th birthday. Well, I didn't believe it. First of all, they were Quakers. Now, what do Quaker pacifists have to do with seagoing robbers, you see, under a son of a bitch like-- [LAUGHTER] --like William of Normandy, I don't believe it. Well, then I got to looking into it, and I looked it up in the British-- Burke's it's called. The same Burke that-- that wrote the Same Burke.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Steer is in there. And there's a coat of arms, and a motto. And there's a-- and two crossed oars on top. Oars. Steersman, see? Steer. Well! Then all of a sudden, you know, I'm being a little more impressed by the old man's talk, see? So I thought, well, if I've got to go in one, I said, let's go back to the family. I'll go in the Navy. And when did you officially become a commissioned officer of the US Navy?

speaker: interviewer
text: At what rank?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ensign, in all his glory. The lowest-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --of the low. Now, at that time, were you single or married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, single.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't get married till after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you were a Quaker.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a Quaker.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you joined the Navy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I joined the Navy. And there was, as I [? say ?] hell to pay. I, I was not a biddable son, friend. I had voted for Roosevelt, that terrible sin to begin with, you see. And then I had-- I had-- Well, the point is this. I said-- I told-- we had big shouting arguments, you know. And I said to my father, I said, you have sent me to the best schools. And I am-- I didn't say you, I said thee. I used the Quaker thee and thou, you know. And they have taught me that I have a good head, and I'm to use it. OK, my good head says that there's a war coming. And my good head said it's a worthwhile thing to fight for. And my good head says I should be there and get prepared in advance. Absolutely zero understanding, zero. Zero.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents ever become acclimated to your military career?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. They didn't-- they didn't throw me out. I was-- I was afraid at one point I was never gonna see home again [LAUGHS] except for short visits. But no, they finally resigned themselves to the fact that I could not be swayed, that I'm just going to go ahead with this. And I remember I went home once [LAUGHS] on leave from the Navy and I spent three or four days at home, I've forgotten how long. Anyway. I was in uniform, of course. And, this was before I shipped out into the Pacific. And I was there over a weekend.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my mother says, well, son, thee will accompany us to Sunday Meeting, will thee not? I said, in uniform? Yes. OK, Mother. Yup. If that's what thee wants, I'll go. And so--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I went to Sunday Quaker Meeting, in uniform, with my parents. So I mean, there was a surface accommodation, but it was uneasy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, where were you posted in the Navy between your commission and Pearl Harbor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Norfolk, Virginia. And I was-- I was in, in intelligence at that point. When I first-- well, let me go back a minute. When the Nazis invaded Poland in 1939, I was in New York City with a girlfriend visiting the World's Fair. And all of a sudden. It came on the radio. I thought, oh God, this is it. I've got to go. And I've been thinking about doing this for years but held back by family objections. Well, I've got to go down. All right. So I went down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Down where, just--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Down to the Philadelphia Navy Yard.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Foot of Broad Street. You know anything about Philadelphia? All right. And I started to say, how do I get into the Naval Reserve? And I started giving my background. They said, stop right there. You want to go into the, the intelligence. I got the languages, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they sent me and I got an intelligence commission. All right, the first thing they did was send me to Norfolk, Virginia. And what did they have me doing but following people around? Nothing to do with German, you see. And I, I was--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] They'd say, well, we suspect so-and-so of having contacts with such-and-such. Now we want to find-- that's FBI stuff. I don't want to do with any of it. So finally I got out of there, and I got a-- got 'em to transfer me to Washington, DC, where there were all sorts of things that did concern me and my language effort. And-- and I-- but then I got [? so-- ?] I had to report to work at 6:30 in the morning in the old Navy Munitions building. It's gone now, but it used to be where the Tidal Basin now is. They've torn the goddamn thing down, it was a hideous thing. And I had to make up-- take all the traffic that came in from the European theater and post it on a big old board four by eight feet. And I would be joined by the-- I was for the Navy, and there was the Army and there's the Air Force and everything. And then we-- at-- at 8:00 we would take this down to the director of Naval Intelligence office and we would brief the old man. Wilkinson. He was a grand old man. And then we would be dismissed. And then he would take our board down to Second Ave, secretary of the Navy, one floor lower. And then-- I mean, Second Ave would take our board and go to the White House and brief FDR. Well, I always wanted to go, I never got to go to the White House. I kept hinting around, well, now, don't you think I ought to, I could-- well, anyway. So that's what I was doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] And where were you when the Japanese bombed Pearl Harbor?

speaker: interviewee
text: What street address? I was-- well, I was on US Highway One in Swarthmore, PA.

speaker: interviewer
text: What we were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were gassing up the car. I'd stopped [LAUGHS] to gas up the car, and my brother was still sitting in the car with the radio going. And I was, with the dipstick in the oil or something. And he stuck his head out the window, he said, the Japs bombed Pearl Harbor. Oh my God. I mean, everybody could tell exactly where they were in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that affect your naval career?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went back to Washington. I was on my way back to Washington from a weekend away. And by the time I got to Washington-- back in those days it was about a three-hour drive. That was a messy drive. I-- I would convince myself, of course, that my services were absolutely essential here in the new war effort. Of course, Ensign. [LAUGHS] They certainly weren't. So I got inside the DC limits and called up the office. Of course, it was Sunday evening, about 5:00 or 6:00. And this very bored duty officer said, well, come in in the morning at normal times and we'll see what to do. [LAUGHS] Big letdown, I wasn't all that important after all.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] And when you did come in, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's-- that's when I had this feeling. And I'll tell you that I was officer in charge of a fleet of file cabinets. I finally got myself shipped out but with great difficulty. I found out that if I was gonna do anything in this man's Navy, I would have to do it myself [INAUDIBLE]. They had me down in Norfolk following people. Well, the hell with that. And so I looked around, I found out where I ought to be, and I pulled a few strings and made some requests. And I got up to, to, to Washington. I was doing better. And then I got this-- in this dead end, you see, with a goddamn whole room full of file cabinets.

speaker: interviewer
text: These Navy officers, full, full four-striped captain, come in to see me and say, what do you have about the, the, the German aircraft capability in Dakar, West Africa? And I would say, sir, I've got a whole file drawer full of stuff. Let me have a precis of it by close of business today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, close of business today was a half hour away, you see. There was [LAUGHS] no way I could do that. And so I would stay there til 8:00 or 9:00--

speaker: interviewer
text: At night. I thought, well, this is for the birds. And I had to be back in by 6:30 in the morning, you see? And I-- I didn't see daylight except through the dirty windows of that goddamn munitions building for months on end.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I gotta get out of here. And so I, I went over to the [? BUPERS, ?] Bureau of Naval Personnel, which ran the assignment of Naval officers. And I found some junior officers over there. And I'd buy 'em Cokes and buy 'em coffee and stuff, and I got on the inside track, so to speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they said, well, there's a program, they're going to send-- they have too many shore-going officers. And they're going to give some-- some special seagoing training, and would you interested? I said, yes! You know? I was, get the hell out of here. And so they put it in. And then the boys followed it through for me. They'd call me up on the phone. They'd say hey, it's going through, it's all right, it's going through. So then all of a sudden, I get this word that it is not going through and I'm going to stay right where I am. Well, I-- what the hell's going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went over there and I found out what had happened. My commanding officer, about three levels up, was a Navy captain who was a son of a bitch. And he had seen-- by the way, this, this Wilkinson, this admiral I used to report to every morning, had given--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me-- let me take a break with you so we can change tapes and get the whole story.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks.
